title: Thinking in Components
intro: React apps are built from components. Judge whether each approach to component design is a good practice or a bad one.

teach.title: Components Are Like LEGO Sets
teach.body: Imagine building a castle out of LEGO. You don't mold the entire castle as one giant piece — you snap together small, standardized bricks. Each brick is simple and reusable. A 2x4 brick can be part of a castle wall, a spaceship hull, or a bridge. React components work the same way: small, self-contained pieces that you combine to build complex interfaces.

teach.title: What Are Components?
teach.body: Components are the building blocks of a React UI. Each component is a self-contained piece that handles its own rendering and logic. Think of them like LEGO bricks: simple on their own, but powerful when combined. The key is deciding **where to draw the boundaries** between components.

teach.title: The Single Responsibility Principle
teach.body: A component should do **one thing well**. If you find yourself describing a component with the word 'and' — like 'it fetches data AND renders a list AND handles filtering' — that component is doing too much. Break it into smaller pieces: a data-fetching wrapper, a list renderer, and a filter control.

teach.title: Components Are Like Kitchen Stations
teach.body: In a professional kitchen, there's a station for grilling, one for salads, one for desserts. Each station does one thing and does it well. If one chef tried to do everything at once, orders would pile up and food would burn. React components work the same way — each one owns a specific job, and together they serve the full experience.

Thinking in components is how professional developers build apps that scale. When you structure your UI well, adding new features (and new revenue streams) to your product takes hours instead of weeks.

Good components are small, focused, and reusable. If a component is hard to name, it probably does too much. If you copy-paste JSX, you probably need a shared component. Start with fewer, larger components and break them apart as complexity grows.

cards.content: Creating a separate <TaskStatusBadge> component that just displays a colored badge based on task status
cards.explanation: This is a small, focused component with a single responsibility. It's easy to reuse, test, and modify. Exactly how components should work.

cards.content: Building one giant <TaskManager> component that contains the task list, the add form, filters, sorting, and the detail view all in a single file
cards.explanation: This 'god component' does way too much. It will be hundreds of lines long, impossible to test in isolation, and painful to modify. Break it into smaller, focused components.

cards.content: Using a <PageLayout> component that accepts children so different pages can share the same header and sidebar structure
cards.explanation: Composition through children is a core React pattern. This keeps your layout consistent while letting each page control its own content.

cards.content: Passing a task object through 6 levels of components just because the deeply nested <DueDate> component needs the due date
cards.explanation: Prop drilling through many levels is a sign of poor component structure. Either restructure your components so <DueDate> is closer to the data, or use context to avoid threading props through intermediate layers.

cards.content: Creating a generic <Button> component with variant props (primary, secondary, danger) instead of separate <PrimaryButton>, <SecondaryButton>, and <DangerButton> components
cards.explanation: A single Button with variants is easier to maintain than three separate components. The API is consistent, and adding a new variant is just a new prop value, not a new file.

cards.content: Before writing any feature code, creating an abstract <DataFetcher>, <FormBuilder>, and <ListRenderer> framework that can theoretically handle any data type
cards.explanation: This is premature abstraction. You don't know what patterns will emerge yet. Build the specific features first (tasks, forms), then extract shared patterns once you see real duplication.

cards.content: Naming your component <TaskListItem> so it's immediately clear it renders one item inside a task list
cards.explanation: Clear, descriptive naming makes your codebase self-documenting. Anyone reading the code instantly knows what this component does and where it lives in the UI hierarchy.
